commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=260cfec1745486add19d3833b05ca71f1ccb99c8)
commands.review_summary:
Adds a pre-entry `agent --local` wall-clock timeout and forces the modern and legacy CLI entrypoints to exit after the command promise resolves.

PR surface: Source +79. Total +79 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence live reproduction was established in this review. The source path and related reports support the hang class, but the PR itself says current-main command proof is still missing.
commands.review_followup: A maintainer needs to decide whether core should accept the forced-exit workaround after the timeout-contract defect and missing real behavior proof are addressed.

target.title: fix(cli): bound local CLI lifetime after completion and finite timeout
target.body:
> **Draft — availability workaround, not a lifecycle fix.** This PR chooses "release the caller" over "let every background handle cleanly drain." That can be the right product tradeoff for `agent --local` automation, but the maintainer should decide whether this belongs in core or should remain an operator-side patch.

## Problem

`openclaw agent --local` hangs indefinitely after completing its work. Background handles — LCM compaction loop, otel exporter flush timers, plugin event loops — keep the Node.js event loop alive after the main command promise resolves.

Related issues: #11843, #12904, #15306, #16460, #18502, #35609, #36388, #61182, #63609, #66936.

PR #70691 (merged 2026-04-23, @Takhoffman) fixed the gateway WebSocket handle, which addressed simple CLI commands. But `agent --local` sessions still hang because plugins (LCM, otel, etc.) hold ref'd handles that survive command completion.

Our earlier attempt split this into per-subsystem context-engine cleanup (#86264, ~500 LOC) and a wall-clock timeout (#86276). That approach reimplemented scheduler internals and broke in subshell contexts. Both PRs are now closed in favor of this simpler combined approach.

**Reproduction:**

```bash
# Hangs indefinitely — must be manually killed:
openclaw agent --local --agent eval-1 --timeout 10 \
  --model ollama-cloud/glm-5.1 -m "Say OK" --json
```

## Change

Two complementary mechanisms covering two failure classes:

### Failure class 1: work completes but process stays alive

**Post-resolve forced exit** — when the CLI command promise resolves, call `process.exit()`. A 3-second `SIGKILL` self-kill timer fires first as a fallback in case `process.exit()` is blocked by `beforeExit`/`exit` event handlers that throw or hang; under normal conditions `process.exit()` runs immediately and the timer never fires.

### Failure class 2: promise never resolves

**Wall-clock safety net** (`agent --local` only) — a synchronous IIFE registered before the CLI entry point. Covers hung commands where the agent runtime, codex app-server, or WebSocket never settles.

- Parses `--timeout N` from `process.argv` (no config import, no async — see tradeoff below)
- Falls back to 600 seconds if `--timeout` not provided
- Arms an `unref()`'d `setTimeout` for `timeout + 30s` — the 30-second grace period lets OpenClaw's own `--timeout` mechanism fire first for clean shutdown
- Exits with code 124 (matches `timeout(1)` convention)
- Only activates for `agent --local` — all other subcommands are unaffected

## Tradeoffs and risks

**600s hardcode vs config-backed timeout.** The wall-clock timer defaults to 600s when `--timeout` is not passed on argv. The "correct" default is `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` from config, which #86276 read via an async `readBestEffortConfig` import. That async import broke subshell/command-substitution contexts by changing event-loop tick order — the root cause of the v1 → v2 rewrite. The 600s hardcode is the tradeoff: it matches the current CLI default but will diverge if the configured default changes. Operators who set a custom timeout already pass `--timeout` explicitly.

**process.exit() skips graceful drain.** This is intentional — the work is done and the user is waiting. But plugins that buffer data (e.g., otel metric exporters) may lose their last flush. The 3-second window between `process.exit()` and `SIGKILL` provides some drain time, but it's not guaranteed.

**Not a lifecycle fix.** The proper fix is for each subsystem to `unref()` or `destroy()` its handles when CLI commands complete. This PR is a workaround that makes `agent --local` usable in automation today.

## Supersedes #86264 and #86276

| Aspect | #86264 + #86276 (closed) | This PR |
|--------|--------------------------|---------|
| Files | 4 (call.ts, run-main.js, context-engine-maintenance, entry.js) | 2 (entry.ts, index.ts) |
| Lines | ~500 | ~80 |
| Approach | Per-subsystem handle cleanup + async config-backed timeout | process.exit() on completion + synchronous argv timeout |
| Fragility | Reimplements scheduler internals, breaks across releases | No internal knowledge required |
| Subshell compat | Broken (async IIFE) | Works (synchronous — needs fresh proof on current main) |

## Testing

We've been running the equivalent dist-level patch in production for 2+ weeks across 17 agents with 4 model providers:

- 31/31 regression suite tests PASS (3 tiers, including 7 agent-dispatch tests that previously hung)
- Zero false timeouts with 600s default + 30s grace
- Clean exit within 3 seconds of command completion in all observed cases

**TODO before marking ready:** Source-level proof on current main with tests and real command output.

Supersedes #86264, #86276. Relates to #66936, extends #70691.
target.author: kaspre
